title: The 'fmt' Package
description: Learn how to format and print text.

slides.title: Meet 'fmt' 🖨️
slides.content:
## Standard Library Magic

Go comes with a massive **Standard Library** – a collection of built-in packages ready to jump into action.

The most famous is the **`fmt`** (pronounced *"fumpt"*) package.
It handles formatting text, reading input, and writing things to the screen.

quizData.question: What is the primary purpose of the 'fmt' package?

options.text: To format text and print to the screen

quizData.explanation: fmt stands for format, and it handles text input and output.

slides.title: Println vs Print 🥊
slides.content:
There are different ways to print:

*   `fmt.Print("Hi")`: Prints exactly what you give it.
*   `fmt.Println("Hi")`: Prints what you give it **AND** automatically hits the "Enter" key to start a new line (`ln` stands for 'line').

Let's test it!
slides.codeSnippet:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
    fmt.Print("First ")
    fmt.Print("Second ")
    
    fmt.Println("Third")
    fmt.Println("Fourth")
}

quizData.question: If you want the next thing printed to appear on a brand NEW line, which function should you use?
quizData.explanation: Println automatically appends a newline character to your output.

slides.title: Capital Letters Matter! ⚠️
slides.content:
Did you notice `fmt.Println` starts with a **capital P**?

In Go, if a function name starts with an **Uppercase Letter**, it means it is *exported* (public) and usable outside of its own package!

If it were lowercase `fmt.println()`, the compiler would throw a fit! We'll learn more about this rule later.

quizData.question: You can use lowercase `fmt.println("Hi")` in your code.
quizData.explanation: False! Because it is exposed from the `fmt` package, it must start with a capital letter: `Println`.
